paragraphs.body: Shit (v. & n.)

Language, like memory, evolves–twisting, decaying, reappearing in new forms, holding weight beyond its surface. Take the word shit: crude, vulgar, overused. But also intimate, complex, and strangely honest. A word that means both nothing and everything, depending on who’s saying it and why.

annotations.text: Language, like memory, evolves–twisting, decaying, reappearing in new forms, holding weight beyond its surface.

paragraphs.body: As a verb, shit is simple–biological. To defecate. To rid the body of waste. But as a noun, it stretches further: excrement, nonsense, disaster, disrespect, intoxication. Trouble. A mess. A feeling. A scream. It emerges from Old English scītan–"to separate, to cut"–tracing back to the Proto-Indo-European root skei- meaning “to split.” In its very origin lies the notion of division. Of something once whole being pulled apart.

That’s what this word has come to mean to me: separation. Loss. Longing. A connection gone to shit. Not just broken, but split–like cells dividing, like a sharp line drawn between two halves that once made a whole.

annotations.text: That’s what this word has come to mean to me

paragraphs.body: It was March 11th. I was on the third floor of the Cathedral of Learning, talking to Dani from Deaf Culture about our presentation next week. My fingers hovered over my MacBook, but my mind had slipped somewhere else. I looked up and froze.

annotations.text: It was March 11th. I was on the third floor of the Cathedral of Learning, talking to Dani from Deaf Culture about our presentation next week.

paragraphs.body: Your dark, shaggy wolfcut caught my eye first, the same haircut I gave you two months ago. My breath hitched. This was the first time I’d seen you since January 23rd. But you weren’t supposed to be here. I had your schedule memorized, a quiet, unspoken preparation in case we ever crossed paths again. You didn’t have classes here today. Yet there you were, standing just a few feet away, your intoxicating laugh threading through the air with another woman. Who was she?

My gaze dropped to your arm, to the vine wrap tattoo that curled around your forearm and stretched to your elbow, ink that had settled into your skin as if it had always been there. On your other arm, just above the elbow, sat the small peppered toad I used to trace absentmindedly, fingertips gliding over the slight ridges where ink met skin.

And then–the shirt. The royal purple tee we bought together from Urban Outfitters, the one we picked out on a whim in the discounted section, laughing as we argued over who would get to wear it first. Ultimately, we decided to get matching ones. You always said it looked better on me, but I remember how it fit you perfectly, the way you cuffed the sleeves just right.

annotations.text: The royal purple tee we bought together from Urban Outfitters, the one we picked out on a whim in the discounted section, laughing as we argued over who would get to wear it first.
annotations.comment: What is the metaphor of the shirt? Let's talk :)

paragraphs.body: I almost wore mine today. The realization tightened around my ribs, a thread pulled too taut. If I had, would you have noticed? Would your gaze have lingered, even for a second? Or would it have meant nothing–just two people who used to share something, now reduced to nothing more than coincidence?

annotations.context_left: The realization tightened around my ribs, a thread pulled too taut. If I had, would you

paragraphs.body: I never even saw your face. But I didn’t need to. I knew it was you from the way you stood, the way your presence filled the space like an echo of something I used to hold.
I swallowed hard and looked away, willing the moment to pass. Twenty seconds, maybe less. But the weight of it stayed, pressing against my chest like a memory that refuses to let go.

I haven’t seen you since. The shirt hangs in my closet–untouched, undisturbed. It still carries the faded scent of lavender Downy dryer sheets, and something else: something heavier, harder to name. The cotton is soft, but suffocating. When I wear it, it clings–not just to my body, but to my memories. It wraps itself around my ribs like a hand pulling me backward. It reminds me of what everything meant before.

There’s an eerie to it now, as though the shirt itself knows too much. It’s just fabric, but it presses on me with a force that makes me feel like a boulder rolling uphill, waiting to be forgotten. Waiting to fall. I wonder when you wear yours, does it haunt you like this? Or did you strip it of meaning, wash it clean, wear it until it became just another piece of clothing?

annotations.text: There’s an eerie to it now, as though the shirt itself knows too much. It’s just fabric, but it presses on me with a force that makes me feel like a boulder rolling uphill, waiting to be forgotten. Waiting to fall. I wonder when you wear yours, does it haunt you like this? Or did you strip it of meaning, wash it clean, wear it until it became just another piece of clothing?
annotations.comment: Aah. I bet the metaphor is somewhere in this paragraph. Also, how does this object, this metaphor, complicate language? The way words shift meaning...the obfuscation of "shit"?

paragraphs.body: I could bury mine under other clothes. But even then, I know it would remain, a folded relic of something unresolved.

Shit has come to mean longing. A longing so specific and visceral it feels like grief. Grief not just for a person, but for the version of myself who believed we were unbreakable. Our connection didn’t just fade–it was severed. I did the severing. You made the separation. Or maybe, you were forced to choose it. And now, all that’s left is shit: the mess we never cleaned up. The ghost in a purple shirt. The silence that still feels louder than anything we ever said.

Over time, the word shit has transformed. From a medical term to literary taboo. From unspeakable to unavoidable. Now it thrives in slang, in pop culture, in the corners of language where feeling is too big for politeness. It’s rebellion. It's an expression. It’s the moment when the “shit hits the fan.” It’s survival. It’s humor. It’s heartbreak. It’s everything we can’t say in clean words.

And somehow, it’s honest. But what even is acceptable anymore? I keep circling that question, like it holds the key to what went wrong. What’s acceptable to say. What’s acceptable to feel. What’s acceptable to grieve. Somewhere along the way, I started editing myself for decency. For dignity. For damage control. Because feelings–raw, messy, unscripted–are somehow too much. And yet not enough. It’s acceptable to say you miss someone, but not to admit you still feel them in your lungs when you breathe. It’s acceptable to move on, but not to revisit the scene of the rupture. It’s acceptable to wear the shirt, maybe. But not to let it mean anything anymore.

annotations.text: I keep circling that question, like it holds the key to what went wrong. What’s acceptable to say. What’s acceptable to feel. What’s acceptable to grieve. It’s acceptable to say you miss someone, but not to admit you still feel them in your lungs when you breathe. It’s acceptable to move on, but not to revisit the scene of the rupture. It’s acceptable to wear the shirt, maybe. But not to let it mean anything anymore.

paragraphs.body: We live in a world that begs for authenticity but only rewards it when it’s clean. When it’s post-processed. Grief must be poetic. Anger must be measured. Love must be mutual. Otherwise, it’s not acceptable. Otherwise, it’s shit.

annotations.text: We live in a world that begs for authenticity but only rewards it when it’s clean. When it’s post-processed. Grief must be poetic. Anger must be measured. Love must be mutual. Otherwise, it’s not acceptable. Otherwise, it’s shit.
annotations.comment: Yes. This is the center of gravity.

paragraphs.body: But I think we’re all just stuck in this pile of shit. This unspoken mess of heartbreak, hope, projection, memory, and fear. We try to climb out, but even our words pull us under.. Every reminder replays the moment. And I wonder–maybe we’re meant to sit here a little longer. To let it stink. To name it. To stop pretending it’s something it’s not.

annotations.text: And I wonder–maybe we’re meant to sit here a little longer. To let it stink. To name it. To stop pretending it’s something it’s not.

paragraphs.body: Because this is what’s real. And real doesn’t have to be acceptable. So what does shit mean? Shit is not just a vulgar word or crude expression. It’s not only excrement or a mess, not just trouble or nonsense. It’s not always something you can wash away, clean up, or ignore. It’s not just a laugh between friends or a curse shouted in traffic. Shit isn’t simple.

To me, shit is separation. It’s the sting of seeing someone you loved across the room, laughing with someone else, wearing the shirt you picked out together. It’s the ache that clings to fabric, memory pressed into cotton. Shit is the ghost of something unresolved, the version of you that still reaches backward. It’s not just grief for a person–it’s grief for a feeling, for a shared timeline that fractured. Shit is the weight of what we can’t say in clean words: heartbreak, longing, the unspoken rupture. It’s honest, too honest for politeness.

We live in it. We name it. We try to hide it under civility, but the truth always finds its way through. Shit is the residue of love and loss, the echo that still fills your lungs. It means we were once whole. Now, we are separate. And I still give a shit.

annotations.text: And I still give a shit.
